title: Peripheral IV Complications: Infiltration, Phlebitis, and Extravasation
subtitle: A nurse monitoring peripheral IV sites must recognize infiltration, phlebitis, extravasation, and infection early, then stop the infusion and respond correctly to protect the patient's tissue and vein.

patient.name: Peripheral IV Complications
patient.admitDate: A nurse assesses peripheral IV sites and must recognize and respond to infiltration, phlebitis, extravasation, and infection.

vitals.temp: Cool, swollen, pale, no blood return

nursesNote: The nurse routinely assesses each peripheral IV site (every 1-2 hours) to catch complications early. Infiltration is leakage of a non-vesicant solution into the tissue — cool, pale, taut swelling with a slowed infusion and no blood return. Extravasation is the same leak with a vesicant drug and can cause blistering and tissue necrosis. Phlebitis is inflammation of the vein — redness, warmth, pain, and a palpable cord along its path. Infection shows purulent drainage, redness, and fever. For any of these the nurse first STOPS the infusion. Infiltration and phlebitis: remove the catheter, elevate, apply the appropriate warm or cool compress, and restart elsewhere. Vesicant extravasation: stop, leave the catheter to follow the extravasation protocol/antidote, and notify the provider. Prevention includes good vein selection (avoiding joints and areas of flexion), secure dressing, and frequent assessment.
providerOrders: Assess each peripheral IV site every 1-2 hours
Recognize infiltration, phlebitis, extravasation, and infection early
Stop the infusion at the first sign of any complication
Remove the catheter for infiltration/phlebitis; follow protocol for vesicant extravasation
Elevate the limb, apply the appropriate compress, and document

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: Which findings suggest an IV complication?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings of a complication.

opts.text: Cool, swollen, pale skin around the site with no blood return
Redness, warmth, and a palpable cord along the vein
A brisk blood return with a pain-free, freely flowing IV
Swelling with a vesicant drug that can cause tissue necrosis
Purulent drainage and fever at the insertion site
A slowed or stopped infusion flow
Infiltration is leakage of a non-vesicant solution into the tissue
Extravasation involves a vesicant drug and risks severe tissue injury
Phlebitis is inflammation of the vein wall
Placing the IV over a joint or area of flexion lowers complication risk
Routine site assessment every 1-2 hours catches problems early
A present blood return always rules out infiltration

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to a cool, swollen, infiltrated site

opts.text: Stop the IV infusion
Remove the peripheral catheter
Elevate the extremity and apply the appropriate compress
Assess circulation, document, and notify as needed
Restart IV access at a different site
Cool, pale, swollen skin around the site
Redness and warmth along the vein
Taut, tight skin with slowed flow and no blood return
A palpable cord and tenderness along the vein path
Blanching and coolness of the surrounding tissue
Throbbing pain with erythema tracking up the vein

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stop the infusion immediately
Disconnect tubing but leave the catheter to aspirate/give antidote per protocol
Notify the provider and follow the extravasation protocol
Mark the area, elevate, and apply the ordered compress
Document the event and reassess the site over time
Assessing the IV site every 1-2 hours
Continuing to infuse through a swollen, cool, painful site
Stopping and removing the IV when phlebitis develops
Increasing the rate to flush past an infiltration
Elevating the extremity after an infiltration
Placing the IV directly over a joint for convenience
Following the extravasation protocol for a vesicant leak
Ignoring a slowed infusion with no blood return

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
